flags.justification: Sec. 1-303 gives the Chairperson broad, largely undefined authority to 'establish such policies, definitions, procedures, and standards' as necessary to implement, interpret, and apply the entire Order — significant interpretive power concentrated in one person despite the Council's nominally broad twelve-department membership.
The Chairperson's broad interpretive authority (Flag 1) has no defined independent review process, despite the otherwise detailed reporting structure elsewhere in the order.
Five required program elements (consumer affairs staff, participation, information, training, complaint handling) all coherently serve one purpose: consumer program enhancement.
Extensive, detailed procedural sequencing (60-day comment periods, 30/90/30-day publication deadlines) gives the program elements real specificity.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
A standing coordination function for an ongoing government need, evergreen by nature.
This creates extensive new public-participation and complaint-handling requirements rather than removing any existing oversight.

Required 60-day public comment periods on draft agency consumer programs, mandatory consumer-participation procedures, and required complaint-handling systems together constitute a genuine, structural external-input mechanism.
Establishes a comprehensive, government-wide consumer-protection coordination apparatus — a significant institutional structure for its era.
Careful coordination design with twelve-department Council representation and use of the existing U.S. Office of Consumer Affairs (Sec. 1-802).

Sec. 1-902(c) allows an agency to be excluded from the order's requirements entirely if it demonstrates to the Chairperson's satisfaction, within thirty days, that its activities have 'no substantial impact upon consumers' — a self-assessment-adjacent exemption category that could be broadly claimed.
